coredata.dc:title: A facile approach to synthesis carbon quantum dots-doped P25 visible-light driven photocatalyst with improved NO removal performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Turkish National Committee for Air Pollution Research and Control. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Turkish National Committee for Air Pollution Research and Control. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Junqi
Zhao, Ran
Xia, Min
coredata.dc:description: The water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and a novel composite photocatalyst CQDs/P25 have been prepared by a one-step electrochemical-microwave method. The experimental result shows that the photocatalytic degradation of NO by this new kind of CQDs/P25 composite reaches 71%, which is approximately 1.65 times than that of the pure P25 at room temperature. The initial concentration of NO pollutants is 10 ppm. The photocatalytic efficiency of the composite catalyst achieves the highest when the content of CQDs is 1%. It can be concluded that the CQDs extend the light absorption range and adjust the band gap of pure P25. The high photocatalytic performance of this new kind of photocatalyst can be mainly attributed to the unique up-conversion fluorescence property, nanometer size effect and surface activity effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NO pollutant
CQDs
P25
Composite catalyst
Photocatalytic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discussion on the mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of P25 and CQDs/P25 photocatalysts.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption curves of pure P25 and CQDs/P25-1 wt%.
(A) Diffuse reflectance spectra of pure P25 and CQDs/P25; (B) The relative electron energy (ahv)2 diagram of pure P25 and CQDs/P25.
PL spectra of pure P25 and CQDs/P25 photocatalysts.
XPS spectra of (A) full spectrum, (B)Ti 2p for pure P25 and CQDs/P25-1.0 wt%, (C)C 1s, and (D) O 1s for CQDs/P25-1.0 wt%.
Oxidation efficiency curve (A) and maximum efficiency (B) of pure P25 and doped with 1%, 3%, 5% CQDs composite in visible light.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: QDs: quantum dots; GQDs: graphene quantum dots; RT: room temperature; FL: fluorescent lamp; XL: xenon lamp; LEDL: LED lamp.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of Turkish National Committee for Air Pollution Research and Control.

head.ce:title: A facile approach to synthesis carbon quantum dots-doped P25 visible-light driven photocatalyst with improved NO removal performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan, 430200, Hubei, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, Hubei, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application, Wuhan, 430200, Hubei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application, Wuhan 430200, Hubei, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan, 430200, Hubei, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and a novel composite photocatalyst CQDs/P25 have been prepared by a one-step electrochemical-microwave method. The experimental result shows that the photocatalytic degradation of NO by this new kind of CQDs/P25 composite reaches 71%, which is approximately 1.65 times than that of the pure P25 at room temperature. The initial concentration of NO pollutants is 10 ppm. The photocatalytic efficiency of the composite catalyst achieves the highest when the content of CQDs is 1%. It can be concluded that the CQDs extend the light absorption range and adjust the band gap of pure P25. The high photocatalytic performance of this new kind of photocatalyst can be mainly attributed to the unique up-conversion fluorescence property, nanometer size effect and surface activity effect.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen fixation is an important chemical process second only to photosynthesis in nature, which is needed to build many other important biomolecules such as nucleic acids and proteins in living organisms (Li et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2014). Lightning converts nitrogen into NO in the air, which is one of the ways of natural nitrogen fixation. NO leads to serious environmental problems and harmful effects on human health (Peng et al., 2017). In order to make better use of NO, it is general to use a catalyst to oxidize NO to NO2 and nitrate. NO2 and nitrate can be used to produce nitric acid and nitrogen fertilizer.

In recent years, photocatalytic oxidation using catalysts has become one of the most promising means for converting NO to NO2 (Jiang et al., 2018a; Martins et al., 2016). Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is one of the most widely-studied semiconductor photocatalysts, because of its excellent capability (Papailias et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019). However, the visible light which accounts for 43% of solar energy can't be utilized by TiO2, because of its large band gap constraint (3.0–3.2 eV) (Ma et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018). The fast photoinduced electron-hole recombination is another main issue for TiO2, which cut the lifetime of active radicals (He et al., 2016). Therefore, modifications of TiO2 have been of great interest to extend its light absorption region and improve its band structure (Avilés-García et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2014b; Xu et al., 2018).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a rising star in the area of quantum dots, because of low cytotoxicity and environmentally friendly (Martins et al., 2016). Based on previous research work, CQDs have been employed to modify semiconductor to enhance their photocatalytic activity with the virtue of excellent upconversion, photoluminescence, and electron-transfer (Chen et al., 2018; Di et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2013). CQDs were first discovered during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by Xu et al. (Bai et al., 2012; Lim et al., 2015). Since then, more and more preparation schemes of carbon dots have been proposed. Among them, the electrochemical method was first proposed by Zhou et al., in 2007 (Zhou et al., 2007). They used carbon nanotubes as the working electrode and applied a certain voltage on both electrodes. The CQDs were stripped from the working electrode. Electrochemical exfoliation has drawn great interest from people because of low cost, and it was the most frequently used method to prepare CQDs with the advantages of high yield, high purity and easy manipulation of size (Wang et al., 2017). Table 1 lists a comparison of NO conversion efficiencies of modified TiO2 and photocatalysts doped quantum dots under visible light. As was shown in Table 1, studies of the photocatalytic oxidation of high concentration NO under visible light activity are still scarce.

In this work, CQDs were prepared by the electrochemical method and combined with P25 by a microwave-assisted method to obtain a novel composite photocatalyst CQDs/P25, which exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic performance of photocatalytic remove ppm-level NO pollutant under visible light. Finally, the possible reaction mechanism of NO oxidation reactions in CQDs/P25 photocatalyst was discussed according to the results of characterization and experimental.

P25 was purchased from Evonik Degussa Specialty Chemicals (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Graphite rod (99.99%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized by electrochemical etching of graphite. During the synthetic process, two parallel graphite rods were inserted 10 cm into the electrolyte at a distance of 7.5 cm, which was used as cathode and anode in a test cell. Ultrapure water was used electrolyte (500 mL). The static voltage of 60 V was applied to the two graphite rods by a source of direct current with continuously stirring. In the following seven days, the solution gradually turns into dark yellow. Subsequently, slow-speed quantitative filter paper was used to filter the dark yellow solution. In order to remove the unreacted graphite particles and the graphite oxide, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 22,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the supernatant was dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight to obtain water-soluble CQDs (Ming et al., 2012).

The CQDs/P25 composites were obtained via a microwave method. Typically, 1.2 g P25 and CQDs powder (0.006, 0.012, 0.036 and 0.060 g) were added into a homogeneous mixed solution containing 20 mL ethanol and 100 mL distilled water. The resultant mixture was stirred for 4 h at room temperature. After that, the suspension was transferred into four 100 mL digestion tanks and maintained in a microwave digestion system at 140 °C for 20 min. After being collected by centrifugation, the obtained CQDs/P25 composites were repeatedly washed in water and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight, finally calcined at 550 °C for 2 h with a ramping rate of 5 °C/min (Yu et al., 2014).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles were recorded on a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer, using Cu Kα radiation at a scanning rate of 10 °/min. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of materials were observed by a Tecnai G220 microscope. Diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was measured by a Hitachi U4100 spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere scanning from 200–800 nm. BaSO4 was used as the reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were detected on a Gangdong F-280 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 150 W xenon lamp as the light source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI using Al Kα radiation as the X-ray source. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained using a TriStar II3020. The total specific surface area of the samples was calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equations.

The diagram of the experiment system was shown in Fig. 1 . The system consists of the gas cylinders, the photoreactor, and the flue gas analyzer. Simulated flue gas: N2/O2/NO = 72%/8%/20%. The NO concentration is 10 ppm. The total flow rate was controlled at 50 mL/min for all testing conditions by the mass flow controller (MFC). The stream passed through a water bubbler to introduce a stable water vapor. The experimental temperature was controlled at 25 °C by the water bath. 0.5 g of the photocatalyst sample was tiled on the reactor for each test. The concentrations of inlet and outlet NO were monitored online by the flue gas analyzer (KM 950, KANE inc, UK) (Gong et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2014). The photoreactor was placed in a photochemical reactor (Shanghai Hannuo Instruments Limited Company, China) in order to prevent the environmental light disturbing. A 150 W xenon lamp (320 nm<λ < 780 nm, PLS-SXE300, Beijing Perfectlight Limited Company, China) was used as light source, which was equipped with an ultraviolet cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm). The NO removal rate was calculated as follows:
(1)
η
N O
=
(
1 − C /
C
0
)
× 100
where ηNO is the conversion rate (%), C stands for the outlet NO concentration (ppm) and C0 stands for the inlet NO concentration (ppm).

Fig. 2 shows the pattern of CQDs/P25 heterostructures. Strong XRD diffraction peaks at (101), (004), (111), (200), (106), (211), (117), (200), (215) and (110), (101), (111), (002) derived from the anatase TiO2 and rutile TiO2, respectively (JCPDS card no. 21–1272, 21-1276). There were no obvious diffraction peaks derived from CQDs, which are probably attributed to their low content and poor crystallinity. After doping CQDs, the intensity of the diffraction peak (101) became weaker than that of pure P25, which indicates that the doping of a small amount of CQDs hinders the formation of anatase particles, which is also consistent with previous studies (Pan et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2014). When the doping amount of CQDs is 5%, the diffraction peak (101) is stronger than that of the sample with a doping amount of 3%, which means that partial CQDs agglomerate due to the excessive doping amount. Besides, the diffraction peak (101, Anatase) has a slight shift to higher 2θ consistent with a slight shrink of the unit cell (Armstrong et al., 2005).

The existence of CQDs in the products could be confirmed by HRTEM observation. As shown in Fig. 3 , the small CQDs appear as spherical particles with a diameter of 10 nm, which were doped on the surface of the TiO2. The lattice spacing of 0.33 nm and 0.785 nm agree well with the (110) spacing of P25 and the (004) spacing of graphite carbon, indicating the good combination of CQDs and P25 (Ma et al., 2013; Pan et al., 2014).

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of P25 and CQDs/P25-1 wt% composites were shown in Fig. 4 . The nitrogen adsorption-desorption curves are all type III weak interaction curves. The adsorption volume is small in the low-pressure region and no inflection point appears which indicates that the force between the catalyst and the adsorbate is rather weak. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area of P25 and CQDs/P25-1 wt% is 60.329 m2g-1 and 66.625 m2g-1, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that the slight surface area enhancement is not the main factor for the higher photocatalytic activity of CQDs/P25-1 wt% (Lim et al., 2015; Martins et al., 2016).

The photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts was intensively influenced by their light absorption capacity. As shown in Fig. 5 A, pure P25 has almost no absorption above 420 nm. As for wavelength range from 420 to 780 nm, the CQDs/P25 composites present an enhanced absorption. This indicates that CQDs/P25 can be excited to generate more electron-hole pairs by the visible light and have higher photocatalytic activity. It can be concluded that the CQDs/P25-1 wt% has the strongest light absorption capability (Pan et al., 2014). A wider light absorption range of the sample means the smaller band gap energy while the sample can absorb more energy from the light. In order to further investigate the effect of doping amount on the absorption edge, the direct band gaps were also estimated according to the relation between (ahv)2 and hv (α = Bd(hν−Eg)1/2/hν). As shown in Fig. 5B, the band gap of CQDs/P25 composites (2.69 eV–2.75 eV) was significantly smaller than the band gap of pure P25 (3.05 eV). It can be deduced that impurity levels were formed in CQDs/P25, which implies that the electrons in the P25 valence band are more easily excited to the conduction band after the CQDs modification. The samples have a wider photoresponse range and can absorb more light energy, which helps to improve the photocatalytic activity (Yu et al., 2006).

The PL spectra of CQDs/P25 composites were carried out to investigate the electron transfer. As Fig. 6 shows, the PL peak at 420 nm was clearly observed for all the CQDs/P25 composites. The peak intensity significantly attenuated in all composites after anchoring the CQDs. It can be concluded that the CQDs can accept the photo-generated electrons transferred from P25 as a photosensitizer, which inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron-holes (Qu et al., 2016).

XPS was performed to further expose the connection between the CDQs and P25. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the full-scale XPS spectrum exhibits the existence of the main elements C, O, and Ti in the CQDs/P25-1.0 wt%. The C1s peak in the full-scale spectrum of P25 should be assigned to the background contaminant (Huang et al., 2008). There is no obvious difference between the full-scale XPS spectrums of CQDs/P25-1.0 wt% and pure P25, which is attributed to the low content and poor crystallinity of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 7B, the peaks at 464.3 and 458.3eV correspond to the binding energy of Ti (2p1/2) and Ti (2p3/2). The peak Ti (2p3/2) in CQDs/P25-1.0 wt% slightly shifted towards the lower binding energy compared with pure P25, which implied that the Ti element in the CQDs/P25-1.0 wt% has changed to a new chemical environment owing to the connection between the P25 and CQDs. As shown in Fig. 7C, the C(1s) XPS spectrum of the CQDs/P25-1.0 wt% can be deconvoluted to three typical peaks at 284.6 (C–C bond), 285.9 (C–O bond) and 288.5eV (CO bond). The peak at the binding energy of 284.6 eV is usually assigned to the functional groups in CQDs and the background contaminant. Fig. 7D shows the deconvoluted O(1s) XPS spectrum, which was fitted into three peaks at 529.3, 530.1 and 531.5eV, they were ascribed to the Ti–O, C–O, and O–H bonds, respectively. The above-mentioned results strongly suggested the existence of the Ti–O–C bond in CQDs/P25 composites, which was also consistent with the results in previous reports. (Yu et al., 2014; Miao et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2009).

The NO photocatalytic oxidation activities of the as-prepared CQDs/P25 samples were tested under visible light. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the NO removal efficiency without photocatalysts was negligible under visible light irradiation. With doping more CQDs, the photocatalytic oxidation rate of NO shows a trend of increasing first and then decreasing. It can be concluded that the CQDs/P25-1 wt% performed the highest evolution rate (71%), which is approximately 1.65 times the performance of the pure P25. The NO oxidation rate shows a declining tendency with further increasing the contents of the CQDs, which is probably due to the obstruction effect of the CQDs to the incident light and the shielding effect to active sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para:
On the basis of the aforementioned analysis, it can be concluded that this is a synergistic process of physical adsorption and chemical catalysis. The main reaction path of NO removal is that NO pollutant was oxidized to NO2 while the NO2 was further oxidized to NO3 − (Li et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2017; Gong et al., 2019). A possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic NO oxidation was proposed in Fig. 9 . First, long-wave excited CQDs emit shorter energy waves due to the nature of the up-conversion luminescence on CQDs (Cao et al., 2007). Second, highly dispersed CQDs possess size effects, resulting in the generation of impurity levels in composite photocatalyst CQDs/P25. The Photoinduced electrons were excited to transfer from the VB of P25 to impurity levels (Eq. (2)), then a small part of photoinduced electrons in impurity levels transfer to the CB of P25 (Lu et al., 2016). Third, since the CQDs can trap most of electrons from the impurity levels and the conduction band, the electron-hole recombination is thus hindered, which help more charge carriers to form reactive species ( ·OH and
·O 2 -
). The photoinduced electrons can be trapped by O2 to form reactive
·O 2 -
(Eq. (3)). Holes can directly oxidize OH− and H2O to ·OH (Eq. (4), Eq. (5)). Besides, the photoinduced holes can also oxidize NO to NO3 − (Eq. (6)~Eq. (8)) (Li et al., 2016a; Wang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). In brief, the CQDs can help improve the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/P25 composite through three ways: widening the light absorption range, improving the charge transfer and inhibiting the electron-hole pair recombination (Martins et al., 2016).
(2)
CQDs / P 25 + hv →
e -
+
h +
(3)
O 2
+
e -
→ ·
O 2 -
(4)
h +
+
H 2
O → · OH +
H +
(5)
OH -
+
h +
→ · OH
(6)
NO + 2 · OH →
NO 2
+
H 2
O
(7)
NO 2
+ · OH →
NO 3
-
+
H +
(8)
NO + ·
O 2
-
→
NO 3
-

In this work, a new composite photocatalyst CQDs/P25 was prepared by electrochemical and microwave-assisted synthesis. The efficiency of photocatalytic conversion of NO by CQDs/P25 under visible light was tested. The results show that when the doping amount of CQDs is 1%, the photocatalytic NO oxidation efficiency of CQDs/P25 is 71%, which is approximately 1.65 times the performance of the pure P25. Possible mechanism of CQDs/P25 photocatalytic activity enhancement are summarized as follows: (1) The up-conversion fluorescence of CQDs broadens the light absorption range of P25, improves the utilization of visible light by P25, and produces more electron-hole pairs; (2) The size effect of CQDs makes the composite CQDs/P25 produce impurity levels, the forbidden bandwidth (2.69 eV–2.75 eV) is significantly narrower than the forbidden bandwidth of the pure P25 (3.05eV), thus the electrons on the valence band are more easily to be excited to the conduction band; (3) The surface effect of CQDs is beneficial for capturing the photogenerated electrons generated by P25, and reduce the electron-hole recombination. This paper contributes to the application of solar energy in environmental protection industries and the development of nitrogen fixation technology in future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Scientific Research Plan Project of Education Department of Hubei Province (Grant D20181702), and Innovation Team of Generation and Control of Air Pollutants in Textile Industry (Grant 201401003).
